summary:
The user, Analyst 3, began their shift to definitively prove the prior session's theory that a TLS certificate issue was the root cause of a major service outage. They established a direct link by matching a certificate serial number from a previous error log to a symptomatic pod, confirming it had an incorrect Subject Alternative Name (SAN). The user then methodically reconstructed the full causal chain: the Certificate Lifecycle Service (CLS) job's "partial_success" deployed 119 bad certs (38.1%), causing mTLS failures, which led to application threads hanging and exhausting the database connection pool. This overturned the initial theory that the DB pool was the root cause, re-framing it as a downstream symptom.

To achieve high confidence, the user ran a quantitative test showing a 38.1% mTLS failure rate, perfectly matching the bad cert ratio. With this proof, they escalated the incident to SEV-0 and oversaw the successful canary deployment of an emergency certificate re-rotation. By the end of the shift, the fix was 46% complete and key metrics were improving. The user resolved all nine open items from previous shifts and created two new red flags for the next shift to investigate the underlying code bug and the "soft-fail" configuration that allowed the incident to occur.
key_facts: The certificate with serial '7A:3B:91:EE:04:C1', seen in earlier TLS errors, was confirmed to be on a symptomatic pod with the wrong SAN ('DNS:*.mesh.internal').
The root cause was a CLS rotation job that deployed 119 of 312 certificates (38.1%) with an incorrect SAN.
A full causal chain was established: Bad Certs -> mTLS Failures -> Thread Hangs/DB Connection Leaks -> PgBouncer Pool Saturation -> Latency Spikes.
A targeted mTLS handshake test showed a 38.1% failure rate, quantitatively proving the impact of the bad certificates.
The incident was escalated to SEV-0 due to the confirmed root cause, high-risk remediation, and significant business impact (e.g., 23 enterprise SLA breaches).
A canary deployment of the fix was successful, with error rates on remediated pods dropping to near-zero and thread counts normalizing.
By the end of the shift, the fix was 46.2% complete, and service health metrics, including the PgBouncer pool size, were showing proportional recovery.
The CLS job that caused the issue completed with a 'partial_success' status and an exit code of 0, which did not trigger an immediate failure alert.
All nine open red flags from Shifts A and B were resolved with specific evidence gathered during the session.
Two new red flags were created for the next shift: C-RF1 (investigate the code bug in PR #4781) and C-RF2 (investigate why SAN validation was in 'soft-fail' mode).
positions_taken: The user expressed high confidence in the root cause after the quantitative test results matched the percentage of bad certificates.
The user stated that the DB pool saturation was a symptom (step 6 in the causal chain), not the root cause.
The user determined that the incident's severity and business impact justified an escalation to SEV-0.
The user identified the combination of a 'soft-fail' validation mode and a 'partial_success' job status with exit code 0 as a dangerous condition and a key contributing factor to the incident.
The user's strategy was to build an evidence-backed, end-to-end causal chain before escalating or beginning remediation.
The user concluded that the two new red flags regarding the code bug and the soft-fail mode were critical for future incident prevention.
positions_changed: The Shift A theory that the DB pool exhaustion was the root cause was definitively overturned; it was re-contextualized as a downstream symptom.
The Shift B theory of a 'TLS certificate issue' was confirmed and specified as a 'CLS SAN mismatch', moving it from a hypothesis to a proven root cause.
